narrative_blocks.header: Invented the first practical lawn mower (1847)
narrative_blocks.detail: In 1847, William Ketchum patented the first practical lawn mower in the United States. In 1861, he patented the Ketchum Hand Grenade — a cast iron oval head charged with gunpowder, an 8-inch wooden tail rod with four pasteboard fins, and a percussion cap nose. Made in 1, 3, and 5 lb sizes, approximately 93,200 were procured by the Union Army. The grenade was used at the Siege of Vicksburg and the Siege of Petersburg. A famous incident at Port Hudson: Confederate defenders learned to catch them in blankets and hurl them back — the grenades often exploded on return with greater force. The 1 and 3 lb sizes were preferred as they could be thrown farther.
narrative_blocks.body: Two inventions from the same man: the first practical U.S. lawn mower patent (1847) and the Ketchum Hand Grenade (U.S. Pat. #33,089, 1861), used across multiple Civil War sieges. About 93,200 were procured by the Union Army.

narrative_blocks.header: 14th Mayor of Buffalo, Millard Fillmore appointee
narrative_blocks.detail: William Ketchum served as Buffalo Village Trustee before the city's incorporation, then on the Erie County Board of Supervisors. He won election as the 14th Mayor of Buffalo on March 5, 1844 as the Whig candidate, serving until 1845 without seeking re-election. In 1851, President Millard Fillmore appointed him Collector of the Port of Buffalo. He co-founded the original Bank of Buffalo. He was a 6th-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker and a great-grandson of Rev. Jonathan Edwards through his mother Lucy Parsons.
narrative_blocks.body: Elected 14th Mayor of Buffalo as Whig candidate March 5, 1844. Appointed Collector of the Port of Buffalo by President Millard Fillmore in 1851.

narrative_blocks.header: Author: history of Buffalo and biography of Mohawk
narrative_blocks.detail: After retiring from the fur and hat business in 1857, William Ketchum devoted himself to history. In 1864 he published An Authentic and Comprehensive History of Buffalo (2 vols.), covering the city's early inhabitants including the Iroquois Six Nations and a sketch of Sir William Johnson. In 1872 he published a Memoir of Chief Joseph Brant (Thayendanegea), the celebrated Mohawk sachem and warrior. The files of the Buffalonian newspaper are archived at the Buffalo and Erie County Historical Society.
narrative_blocks.body: Wrote a 2-volume history of early Buffalo (1864) and a memoir of Mohawk chief Captain Joseph Brant (1872).

A famous incident at Port Hudson: Confederate defenders learned to catch them in blankets and hurl them back — the grenades often exploded on return with greater force. The 1 and 3 lb sizes were preferred as they could be thrown farther.

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary militia private; son-in-law of Jonathan Edwards

narrative_blocks.header: He marched in the Berkshire County militia
narrative_blocks.body: Elihu Parsons served as a private in the Berkshire County militia during the Revolution, under Captain Ezra Whittlesey and Colonel John Brown. He is recognized as a patriot ancestor by the Daughters of the American Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a daughter of Jonathan Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: In 1750 Parsons wed Sarah Edwards, a daughter of the theologian Jonathan Edwards, and the couple raised eleven children at Stockbridge. He lived as an esquire in the western Massachusetts hills.

sources.label: Salt of the Earth — David Brainerd and Jerusha Edwards: A Colonial Love Story

cross_connections.notes: The coincidence of two daughters marrying two Ingersolls in the same period and region (Berkshire County MA) likely reflects the prominence of the Ingersoll family in that area. (DAR records; FindAGrave)
cross_connections.display_label: Daughter Lydia married Aaron Ingersoll of Berkshire

cross_connections.notes: The coincidence of two daughters marrying two Ingersolls in the same period and region (Berkshire County MA) likely reflects the prominence of the Ingersoll family in that area. (DAR records; FindAGrave)
cross_connections.display_label: Daughter Sarah married David Ingersoll of Berkshire

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Edwards Parsons (1728-1805), eldest daughter of Jonathan Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest Edwards child born Sabbath afternoon, 1728
narrative_blocks.body: She was born August 25, 1728 in Northampton — 'on a Sabbath day, between 2 and 3 o'clock in the afternoon,' as her father Jonathan Edwards recorded. She was the eldest of his eleven children.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her father's attorney — Stockbridge farmer
narrative_blocks.body: In 1750 she married Elihu Parsons, a Northampton attorney who handled many of Jonathan Edwards's legal and personal affairs. Parsons later farmed in Stockbridge, where the family settled during the Mohican mission years.

narrative_blocks.header: Father warned suitor of her temper
narrative_blocks.body: When Elihu Parsons asked Jonathan Edwards for her hand, Edwards reportedly disclosed her difficult temper before quipping: 'I hope she has grace, but grace can live where you cannot.'

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters married into the Berkshire Ingersolls
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her eleven children — both daughters — married into the Ingersoll family of Berkshire County, Massachusetts. This is a separate Ingersoll line from the Loyalist New Haven Ingersolls who descended through the Newton-Miles line.
